artifacts.content:
Hi Mom,

Finally sitting down with some tea while the kids read in the living room, so I wanted to really explain what’s been going on with Eleanor and all this MCA stuff.

You remember last spring’s reading MCA at Rosewood, in Mrs. Keller’s room? That was when she had the full-blown panic attack. Ms. Ramirez walked her down to the nurse’s office because she was shaking and crying so hard she said she couldn’t breathe. The nurse ended up calling Dr. Laura Hendricks at Fairview Clinic–Roseville from her office, and we talked about how the test environment seemed to be the primary trigger. Dr. Hendricks adjusted Eleanor’s sertraline dosage on April 19, 2024, and we spent the rest of that week just trying to get her to sleep through the night again.

The hardest part is that this wasn’t the first time. Back in May 2023 during the math MCA, the school counselor, Mr. Brian Lewis, called me because Eleanor was saying things like “I hate myself, I’m stupid” and scratching her left forearm until there were red marks. He documented it in her file and told me he was worried about depression on top of the anxiety. We ended up at Washburn Center for Children in Minneapolis that same afternoon, May 11, 2023, for an emergency intake.

Day-to-day she’s still our sweet, book-obsessed kid—right now she’s curled up with another “Wings of Fire” book and trying to talk Oliver into reading it too. Pottery class at the community center is a huge bright spot; she’s already planning what color glaze to use on the next mug for you.

I know all of this sounds heavy, but I also want you to know she’s learning coping tools and we’re trying to keep life as normal and cozy as possible. Pancakes on Saturdays, walks around Lake Josephine, Target runs—those little rituals really help.

Love,
Melissa

[Text thread – Melissa & friend “Jen – Rosewood Moms”]

Jen: How’s your crew handling all the “MCA countdown” hype this week?

Me: Ugh, it’s already starting here. Eleanor gets this look on her face every time someone says “test practice.” I’m seriously opting her out this year.

Jen: Really? Because of the anxiety stuff?

Me: Yeah, that, and honestly it’s just less chaos for us if she can stay home on those days instead of dealing with the whole testing circus.

Me: Plus, remember how in Spring 2023 she kept throwing up on the mornings she was supposed to test? We ended up at Children’s Minnesota – St. Paul three different half-days while the MCA window was going on. Dr. Patel ordered bloodwork and an abdominal ultrasound on April 6, 2023 to check for GI issues because she had so many stomachaches.

Jen: Oh wow, I didn’t realize it was that intense.

Me: Yeah. In the end they said it was stress-related functional abdominal pain, not something structurally wrong, which is good but also maddening because it’s clearly tied to school stress.

Jen: That’s awful. Meanwhile, Max just eats the homework.

Me: Ha! Max mostly just steals socks here. Anyway, I’d rather spend that day letting her read and maybe take Max for a walk around Lake Josephine than force her back into that environment.

Jen: Makes total sense. Want to grab coffee after drop-off Thursday?

Me: Yes please. I’ll need it after wrestling Oliver into boots.

Event: Eleanor – CBT with Dr. Megan O’Donnell (test anxiety)
Date: Tuesday, 2026-01-13
Time: 4:00–5:00 p.m.
Location: Roseville Family Counseling
Attendees: Eleanor, Melissa

Notes:

Weekly 60-minute CBT session with Dr. Megan O’Donnell. We’ve been doing this every Tuesday at 4:00 p.m. since January 2025, and it really seems to help Eleanor put words to the test-related anxiety and the school avoidance that crept in after that ugly incident back in May 2022, when a classmate made fun of her MCA score.

Questions to bring up this week:
- How to handle the ramp-up to the 2025–2026 MCA window without Eleanor spiraling.
- Whether skipping the MCAs this year might actually reduce her overall anxiety or if we should be practicing coping in smaller, lower-stakes tests instead.
- Ideas for talking to Mr. Larson about giving her short breaks during math so it doesn’t feel like another timed test.

Logistics:
- Pick Eleanor up from Rosewood Elementary at 3:30 (tell her teacher in the morning).
- Bring her “Wings of Fire” book so she can read in the waiting room.
- Pack a small snack in the car—she’s always starving by 3:45.

General reminder to self: After therapy, keep the evening low-key—simple dinner, maybe a family Mario Kart round with Oliver, then reading under blankets by 8:00. Avoid bringing up grades or tests on therapy days.

Subject: Spring stuff + Eleanor update

Hey Dan,

Figured I’d send you a brain dump while you’re on the Green Line and before the evening chaos starts.

I talked to Mr. Larson after school about spring. They’re already planning the schedule for the Spring 2026 MCA window, and I told him I’m planning to opt Eleanor out again. We talked through the history: the math MCA in May 2023 when Mr. Brian Lewis had to pull her because she was saying “I hate myself, I’m stupid” and scratching up her left forearm, and then that same-day emergency intake at Washburn Center for Children in Minneapolis on May 11, 2023. Plus last spring’s reading MCA in Mrs. Keller’s room, when Ms. Ramirez ended up walking her to the nurse’s office and calling Dr. Hendricks at Fairview Clinic–Roseville, which led to the sertraline dosage change on April 19, 2024.

I also mentioned all the therapy work she’s been doing with Dr. Megan O’Donnell at Roseville Family Counseling—every Tuesday at 4:00 p.m., 60-minute CBT sessions specifically about test anxiety and not wanting to go to school. Mr. Larson seemed genuinely relieved we’re being proactive.

On a more everyday note: Oliver has decided he “hates” carrots again, so dinner was a negotiation. Max stole one of Eleanor’s socks and paraded it around like a trophy. Eleanor is re-reading “Wings of Fire” book 6 and trying to convince Oliver to start at book 1.

I’m thinking pancakes and a Lake Josephine walk Saturday morning if the weather holds. Maybe we can swing by Target afterward for snacks and let the kids look at Legos.

Text me when you’re close so I can start the pasta.

Love,
M

Title: Sorting out my thoughts about MCAs
Date: 2025-12-02

I keep going in circles about whether to just opt Eleanor out of the MCAs again this year, so I’m writing it all down.

The big things:

1) Spring 2023: During the math MCA at Rosewood, Mr. Brian Lewis called because Eleanor was repeating “I hate myself, I’m stupid” and scratching her left forearm hard enough to leave red marks. That led straight to the emergency intake at Washburn Center for Children in Minneapolis on May 11, 2023 for an anxiety/depression evaluation.

2) Spring 2023 again: During the MCA window she missed three half-days of school for appointments at Children’s Minnesota – St. Paul. Dr. Patel ordered bloodwork and an abdominal ultrasound on April 6, 2023 to rule out GI issues because she had stomachaches and vomiting on testing mornings. In the end they called it stress-related functional abdominal pain.

3) Spring 2024: Reading MCA in Mrs. Keller’s class, panic attack. Ms. Ramirez took her to the nurse’s office, called Dr. Laura Hendricks at Fairview Clinic–Roseville, and we adjusted her sertraline on April 19, 2024.

4) Since January 2025 she’s been in weekly 60-minute CBT sessions every Tuesday at 4:00 p.m. with Dr. Megan O’Donnell at Roseville Family Counseling, mainly because of test anxiety and school avoidance after that classmate mocked her MCA score in May 2022.

And honestly, I know the scores supposedly help with advanced placement and all that, but I don’t really care if she misses out on some group or even slips a bit academically because she skips the tests. Her mental health feels more important than a percentile.

Keep coming back to this: is the test worth even the possibility of going through all that again? My gut says no.

Subject: Quick update on Eleanor + summer ideas

Hi Susan,

I wanted to send a longer update now that things have calmed down a bit after conferences.

Overall, Eleanor is doing really well in Mr. Larson’s class—tons of praise for her reading and writing, and he said her cardboard “sustainable city” project was one of the most thoughtful in the group. Math is still a stress point, especially anything timed.

You already know some of the backstory, but I filled him in on why we’re skipping the MCAs again. The big pieces are the panic attack during the Spring 2024 reading MCA in Mrs. Keller’s classroom, when Ms. Ramirez walked her to the nurse’s office and called Dr. Laura Hendricks at Fairview Clinic–Roseville, which led to the sertraline dosage adjustment on April 19, 2024. And then before that, the May 2023 math MCA incident when the counselor, Mr. Brian Lewis, documented that she was saying “I hate myself, I’m stupid” and scratching her left forearm, and we ended up at Washburn Center for Children the same day, May 11, 2023, for an emergency intake.

We also talked about how, since January 2025, she’s been going every Tuesday at 4:00 p.m. for 60-minute CBT sessions with Dr. Megan O’Donnell at Roseville Family Counseling to help with test-related anxiety and the school avoidance that started after a classmate made fun of her MCA score back in May 2022.

On the fun side: we’re looking at a week-long camp at Como Park Zoo & Conservatory that focuses on animal habitats and conservation, and maybe a few days up near Two Harbors in August if Daniel can get the time off. Eleanor is already daydreaming about rocky beaches and the possibility of seeing the northern lights.

Hope you’re doing well—maybe we can plan a Sunday visit soon.

Love,
Melissa

metadata.subject: Quick update on Eleanor + summer ideas

artifacts.content:
[Text thread – Melissa & “Maya’s Mom – Priya”]

Priya: Hey! Are you doing anything special to get Eleanor ready for MCAs? Maya’s teacher keeps sending practice links.

Me: Honestly? I think we’re going to skip them again. She just doesn’t handle long tests well, and I’m tired of being the bad guy pushing her to slog through something she finds boring and miserable.

Priya: That makes sense. I remember you saying she had a rough time last year.

Me: Yeah. And it’s been building for a while. During the Spring 2023 MCA window she had so many stomachaches and vomiting spells on testing mornings that we ended up at Children’s Minnesota – St. Paul three half-days. Dr. Patel even ordered bloodwork and an abdominal ultrasound on April 6, 2023 to rule out GI stuff. It turned out to be stress-related functional abdominal pain.

Priya: Oh wow. Poor kid.

Me: Then in Spring 2024, during the reading MCA in Mrs. Keller’s class, she had a full panic attack. The nurse, Ms. Ramirez, called our pediatrician, Dr. Hendricks at Fairview Clinic–Roseville, from her office. That’s when we adjusted her sertraline dose on April 19, 2024.

Priya: That’s a lot for a ten-year-old.

Me: Exactly. I’d rather she spend that energy on pottery class and reading “Wings of Fire” than on bubble sheets.

Priya: Totally fair. Maybe we can plan a low-key playdate on one of the testing days for the kids who are home.

Me: I love that idea—board games and snacks at our place?

Subject: Drafting our MCA refusal letter tonight

Hi Dan,

I wanted to get my thoughts straight before we send the MCA refusal form back to Rosewood Elementary School.

The biggest thing for me is still what happened last year. After Eleanor’s 2023 math MCA score was flagged as “borderline” in that internal email from Assistant Principal Karen Lee, they quietly pulled her from her regular art class and stuck her in that 6-week small group MCA “boot camp” in Room 214 with Ms. Harris. From February 3, 2024 through March 14, 2024 she missed art every single week, and it really wore on her.

Eleanor keeps saying she felt singled out and embarrassed, especially when other kids asked why she was leaving class with the “math group.” It’s frustrating because she’s a thoughtful fourth grader who already worries too much about getting things right. Losing that creative outlet right before testing season just ramped up her anxiety.

So in the refusal letter, I’m planning to say something like: “We are refusing the MCAs this year because the emphasis on test scores has already led to Eleanor being pulled from her regular art class for a 6-week small-group MCA ‘boot camp’ in Room 214 with Ms. Harris from February 3 to March 14, 2024, after her 2023 math MCA score was flagged as ‘borderline’ in an internal email to us from Assistant Principal Karen Lee, which made Eleanor feel singled out and embarrassed.”

Can you read that over after the kids are in bed and tell me if you want anything added before I send it to Mr. Larson and cc the office?

– Melissa

metadata.subject: Drafting our MCA refusal letter tonight
